name: Pumi
description: The Pumi is a lively and versatile Hungarian herding dog with a distinctive curly or wavy coat and whimsical semi-erect ears that give it a unique, animated expression. Developed in the 17th and 18th centuries by crossing the Puli with German and French sheepdogs, the Pumi became a distinct breed well-suited to herding cattle and pigs in addition to sheep. Energetic and highly trainable, they excel in dog sports and need active owners who can channel their considerable drive.

conditions_detail.name: Hip Dysplasia
conditions_detail.description: Hip dysplasia is a genetic condition where the hip joint doesn't develop properly, causing the ball and socket to not fit together correctly. This leads to joint deterioration, pain, and arthritis over time.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Treatment ranges from weight management and physical therapy for mild cases to total hip replacement for severe cases. NSAIDs and joint supplements help manage pain.
prevention_tips: Screen breeding stock with hip scoring

conditions_detail.name: Degenerative Myelopathy (DM)
conditions_detail.description: DM is a progressive, non-painful spinal cord disease caused by a mutation in the SOD1 gene. It begins with hind limb weakness and ataxia, progressing to complete paralysis and eventually fore limb involvement. It is analogous to ALS in humans.
symptoms: Loss of bladder and bowel control
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: There is no cure or disease-modifying treatment. Intensive physiotherapy and hydrotherapy can slow decline and maintain quality of life. Wheelchairs improve mobility in later stages.
prevention_tips: DNA test available (OFA) — avoid breeding at-risk (AT/AT) pairings

conditions_detail.name: Patellar Luxation
conditions_detail.description: Patellar luxation occurs when the kneecap slips out of its normal groove in the femur. It can be medial (inward) or lateral (outward) and ranges from grade 1 (occasional slipping) to grade 4 (permanent dislocation).
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Grade 1-2 cases are managed conservatively with weight control and physiotherapy. Grade 3-4 typically require surgical correction to deepen the patellar groove and realign the tibial crest.
prevention_tips: Breed from dogs with normal patellar scores
Avoid high-impact activities in affected puppies
